ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Schezwan/Sichuan chicken fried rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried rice as a category. No specific Schezwan chicken fried rice code exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Schezwan chicken fried rice. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual dish variants at this specificity.

main_ingredients: Schezwan sauce (chilli, garlic, soy sauce, vinegar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Schezwan Fried Rice with Chicken is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a heaped plate of spicy fried rice with visible chicken pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 27054) adapted with Schezwan sauce composition from USDA Foundation Foods chicken and condiment entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Schezwan chicken fried rice entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat (NDB 27054) as the primary anchor, with sodium adjusted upward to reflect Schezwan sauce (high soy sauce and chilli paste content). Saturated fat and fat values are consistent with stir-fried rice with chicken. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice-based fried rice analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Schezwan chicken fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from white rice-based fried rice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. White rice GI is approximately 72; the addition of protein (chicken), fat (oil), and fibre from vegetables and sauce moderates the GI to approximately 64, consistent with published mixed fried rice GI values (range 56–72). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 82.25) / 100 = 52.7, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 23.5 × 3.5 = 82.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Schezwan sauce, soy sauce, and chilli paste
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving of Schezwan Fried Rice with Chicken provides approximately 1820 mg sodium, which exceeds 75% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (64) and high glycemic load per serving (52.7) due to white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI and high glycemic load of this dish can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g), pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, or substitute with brown rice where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from oil and chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 4.2 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and choose skinless chicken preparations with reduced oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Schezwan Fried Rice with Chicken is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish widely served across Singapore, India, and Southeast Asia. It consists of cooked white rice stir-fried at high heat in a wok with diced or shredded chicken, egg, spring onion, garlic, ginger, and a bold Schezwan sauce made from dried red chillies, garlic, soy sauce, vinegar, and Sichuan peppercorns. The dish is characterised by its fiery, tangy, and umami-rich flavour profile with a slightly smoky aroma from wok hei. The texture combines fluffy, separated rice grains with tender chicken pieces and a glossy, spicy sauce coating. Despite its name referencing Sichuan (Szechuan) cuisine, the dish as served in Singapore and Indian-Chinese restaurants is an adaptation that uses locally available ingredients and tends to be spicier and more sauce-forward than its Chinese counterpart. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 612 kcal, 29.8 g protein, 18.2 g fat, and 82.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 1820 mg per serving due to soy sauce and Schezwan paste, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated 64) and high glycemic load (52.7) are relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and sauces, and is dairy-free but contains gluten from soy sauce.

What is the difference between Schezwan and regular fried rice?

confidence_reason: No exact Schezwan chicken fried rice entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat (NDB 27054) with sodium adjusted for Schezwan sauce composition. GI is an analogous estimate from published white rice-based fried rice GI values. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried rice with meat (NDB 27054); sodium increased to approximately 520 mg/100g to reflect Schezwan sauce (soy sauce and chilli paste). 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 5. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible in stir-fried rice preparations using vegetable oil per USDA data. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white rice-based fried rice entries; no direct measurement exists for Schezwan chicken fried rice. 7. The input record listed cuisine as 'Turkish' and restaurants as Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine; however, the dish name 'Schezwan Fried Rice Chicken' is unambiguously an Indo-Chinese dish. The cuisine field has been corrected to 'Indo-Chinese'. The field dataing is preserved in source metadata. 8. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation at halal-certified Singapore restaurants; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 9. food_group corrected from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' as the primary component is rice.
